data.title: Astria Therapeutics: A High-Conviction Bet On HAE
data.description:
Small-cap biotech shares can be volatile, but Astria Therapeutics (NASDAQ:ATXS) has a particularly attractive risk-reward profile that the market seems to be underestimating. Astria has entered Phase 3 trials for its hereditary angioedema lead candidate Navenibart (STAR-0215) and has designed a second program, STAR-0310 for atopic dermatitis. The company is poised for an important inflection point. Yet what sets it apart in today's market is its healthy cash position relative to its burn rate, a factor that could give it an extended runway when many small biotechs are struggling to raise money without dilutions.

Financials

Astria’s financial state is a hidden strength in a biotech landscape where liquidity concerns are a major headwind. It had $344.3 million in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments as of September 30, 2024, up from $246.5 million as of 2023. Those cash reserves give Astria something many clinical-stage biotech companies lack: flexibility. While capital markets have tightened and secondary offerings smother share prices, Astria is positioning itself to fund operations through mid-2027 without needing to raise additional cash immediately.

Net operating cash burn of $28 million was reported in Q3 2024, with an annualized burn rate of $112 million. Simple math says Astria has about three years of runway at its current burn rate, assuming no material change in expenses. This is well above the expected Phase 3 readout for Navenibart in early 2027, a key inflection point for the company. Expenses may rise as trials progress, but the existing cash balance buffers dilution risk for investors - something that's not so for many of its small-cap biotech peers competing for funding. The last few months have, for the most part, been consistent in developments with the past. Astria continues R&D, and there have been no new surprises in terms of unforeseen expenses. In about three weeks, the company will release the official number, but I imagine their burn rate should be relatively consistent.

The company is losing money despite that cushion. For Q3 2024 Astria posted a $24.5 million net loss, up from $17.7 million in Q3 2023. This increase in cost is mostly attributed to the Phase 3 preparations for Navenibart and the ongoing STAR-0310 program, including manufacturing and trial initiation. It is necessary, but it means the company growth thesis only remains valid if it can complete its pivotal trials successfully. The upside here is that management has targeted spending efficiently - the vast majority going to R&D rather than administrative bloat. Unlike some biotech firms that see disproportionate general and administrative costs as they scale, Astria's G&A expenses grew only from $6.9 million to $8.5 million year-over-year while remaining focused on clinical execution.

Drug Pipeline

Astria's pipeline centers on two monoclonal antibody programs, Navenibart for hereditary angioedema and STAR-0310 for atopic dermatitis. Both have market potential, but Navenibart is the clear near-term value driver. In the Phase 3 ALPHA-ORBIT trial, which begins in Q1 2025, dosing arms are designed to be every three months and every six months, a patient-centric strategy that may represent the most convenient approach to prophylactic HAE treatment on the market. Early results from the Phase 1b/2 ALPHA-STAR trial demonstrated a 90-95% reduction in mean monthly attack rates and a 67% attack-free rate at six months, suggesting best-in-class potential. These results along with positive pharmacokinetic and safety data make this pivotal study likely to succeed. Although there are competitors in the HAE space, current treatments require more frequent dosing or lack long-term data matching Navenibart's profile, so differentiation may be possible if Phase 3 confirms prior results.

A Phase 1a trial of STAR-0310 began in January 2025 to evaluate safety and pharmacokinetics in healthy adults in early-stage clinical development. This asset targets OX40, which is a key pathway in atopic dermatitis and other inflammatory diseases, and is targeted as a potential best-in-class therapy with YTE half-life extension technology. With lower antibody-dependent cell toxicity than a competitor in Phase 3, rocartinlimab, the company believes it will lead to a better safety profile and therapeutic window. It is too early to make Star-0310 a real value proposition, but initial proof-of-concept data expected in Q3 2025 may act as an upside catalyst if early differentiation becomes clear.

Scientific Background and Approach

Excessive bradykinin production causes hereditary angioedema, a rare genetic disorder characterized by severe, unpredictable swelling attacks. Attacks on the airway may be life-threatening and require prophylactic treatment. Currently, available therapies either replace the C1-inhibitor, which regulates kallikrein activity, or directly inhibit kallikrein to prevent bradykinin overproduction.

The monoclonal antibody against plasma kallikrein, Navenibart, is a long-lasting inhibitor that requires minimal dosing. Unlike other inhibitors that require biweekly or monthly administration, Navenibart's extended half-life permits dosing every three or six months, reducing the treatment burden. Drug durability has improved with advances in antibody engineering without compromising efficacy - an important issue in patient adherence.

Atopic dermatitis is characterized by itching, redness and persistent lesions caused by T-cell dysfunction and barrier disruption. OX40 signaling is a key pathway, which enhances T-cell activation and survival, in memory T-cells of chronic inflammation.

Astria's second asset, STAR-0310, is an OX40 antagonist monoclonal antibody that blocks excessive T-cell activation while maintaining normal immune function. With YTE (Xtend) half-life extension technology, it requires less frequent dosing than most other OX40 inhibitors. Its reduced antibody-dependent cell cytotoxicity suggests a safer profile with lower off-target toxicity - an issue that has challenged some immune-modulating therapies in dermatology.

Phase 3 Strategy Signals Confidence in Navenibart

The ALPHA-ORBIT Phase 3 trial design represents a step toward regulatory approval and market positioning for Astria. Three-month and six-month dosing regimens will be tested in the study, which supports the company's contention that dosing flexibility could provide a competitive advantage over more frequently administered treatments.

This randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial will enroll 145 patients with Type 1 or Type 2 HAE across global sites. Time-normalized monthly attack rates at six months are the primary endpoint, with attack-free rates as a secondary measure. These metrics mirror real-world treatment goals and increase the likelihood of regulatory approval if results are similar to those from prior trials. Open-label extension will allow patients to continue treatment, provide long-term safety and efficacy data, and ensure trial retention.

The trial will start in Q1 2025 with top-line results by early 2027. Although the timeline is long, two dosing regimens and a robust statistical analysis plan suggest a focus on regulatory strength over speed to market. This study was developed with input from the FDA and EMA indicating its global approval potential.

If navenibart can maintain a 90-95% reduction in attack rates with twice-yearly dosing, it could dramatically change treatment preferences. For patients and physicians, a lower treatment burden often determines market leadership - and Astria is betting on that trend.

STAR-0310 Enters Trials With an Eye on Differentiation

The launch of STAR-0310 by Astria is its first major step into the competitive field of atopic dermatitis therapies in which biologics are increasingly redefining treatment paradigms. STAR-0310 competes with established players such as Sanofi's Dupixent (SNY) and a wave of new immunomodulators, unlike Navenibart, which operates in a relatively defined niche market. A big differentiator is in its mechanism of action and extended half-life, two features it says could provide both efficacy and convenience advantages over current options.

The OX 40 pathway, which STAR-0310 targets, is critical for T-cell survival and chronic inflammation, and thus a natural target for AD driven by dysregulated immune responses. Its unique YTE half-life extension technology makes STAR-0310 a long-term active agent in the body, which may result in less frequent dosing than competitors. Such a difference may be meaningful as patients and providers increasingly seek out therapies that reduce treatment burden. Preclinical data also indicates lower antibody-dependent cell-mediated cytotoxicity of STAR-0310 than rocatinlimab, another OX40 antagonist in Phase 3. This discrepancy could lead to a better safety profile and a larger therapeutic window, two factors that have often determined the commercial viability or failure of immunotherapies in dermatology.

Safety, pharmacokinetics, and immunogenicity in 40 healthy adult participants are being evaluated in the Phase 1a trial, which began in January 2025. This early-stage study is not designed to demonstrate efficacy but will provide important information about how the drug behaves in the human body that will help inform future trials in AD patients. With the guidance of early proof-of-concept data in Q3 2025, Astria has outlined a gradual but deliberate development.

Balancing Innovation With Execution Risks

The success of Navenibart is dependent on maintaining near-complete kallikrein inhibition with minimal dosing, but the translation of early-stage data into clinical benefit and regulatory approval remains unclear. While Phase 1b/2 data suggested a 90-95% reduction in attack rate, larger trials typically exhibit variability in response, unexpected safety issues, or regulatory hurdles. The placebo control of the ALPHA-ORBIT trial should clarify if these attack-free rates are valid, but HAE is a highly individual disease. Any deviation from prior efficacy benchmarks would erode its positioning. Antibody-based therapies also involve immunogenicity risk, and although Navenibart showed a good profile, long-term tolerability remains a question.

The challenge for STAR-0310 lies in differentiation in a competitive atopic dermatitis market. AD is regulated by multiple inflammatory pathways, so blocking OX40 alone may not provide broad disease control. Directly targeting key cytokines such as IL-4/IL-13 inhibitors like Dupixent already dominate the space. Other investigators argue that OX40 plays a more restricted role in AD and that STAR-0310 may benefit only a subset of patients rather than being a universal therapy. The YTE half-life extension may allow less frequent dosing but prolonged drug exposure may limit flexibility in case of safety issues.

Of course, both programs also face commercial viability risks. The HAE is a defined rare disease market whose size limits potential sales, and insurers will dictate the uptake of Navenibart. If those who pay see only minor advantages over existing treatments, they may impose access restrictions. In AD, STAR-0310 must outperform existing biologics and also prove its place in an evolving market. Skepticism about the role of OX40 in AD indicates that STAR-0310 may be effective, but will not replace current standard treatments unless safety and dosing benefits are compelling.

Valuation and Market Share

In terms of raw numbers and metrics, the company has a price-to-book ratio of 1.62 compared to the sector median of 2.23, which is a good sign in terms of the company’s financial management despite being early stage. Other than this, however, Astria has very little to show in the way of meaningful financial ratios. This is because the company has no products that have reached market yet, so there is no actual revenue from its products yet. For biotech companies at this stage, their potential value is largely determined by two factors: the science behind the drugs, and the total addressable market if successful. While I have extensively discussed the former, I will estimate the latter below.

About 1 in 50,000 people have HAE. For the US, which has about 330 million people, that amounts to roughly 6,600 people with HAE. HAE is also very economically burdensome. A prior study analyzing data before 2008 reported an average annual cost per patient of $44,597, including direct medical costs and indirect costs like lost productivity. This works out at about $65,000 annually per patient adjusted for inflation through 2025. Newer analyses show that modern HAE therapies can be quite expensive. Hence, one study reported annual medication costs of $363,795 per patient, excluding additional indirect costs of $52,576.

From these figures, the total addressable market for HAE treatments in the U.S. can be estimated. From the estimated 6,600 patients with a $363,795 average annual treatment cost, we arrive at a TAM of approximately $2.4 billion annually, although Astria themselves estimate it higher at about $2.8 billion. This estimation is for the US market only; With regard to the global prevalence of HAE, the worldwide market potential is much greater.

Astria Therapeutic's lead candidate, Navenibart, aims to compete in this market by providing a potentially best-in-class prophylactic treatment for HAE. The suggested dosing regimen of once every three to six months could be advantageous compared to other therapies whose dosage is more frequent. If Navenibart shows superior efficacy and safety in its Phase 3 trials, it may become a preferred treatment option and potentially capture a large share of the HAE therapeutic market.

Takeaways and Outlook

Astria Therapeutics combines clinical ambition with financial discipline in a biotech sector where many companies struggle to achieve both. Its lead candidate, Navenibart, has a clear regulatory path, a defined market, and a dosing advantage that could reshape the treatment of hereditary angioedema if Phase 3 results confirm early data. The three-month and six-month dosing regimens represent a patient-centric approach that if successful may increase market penetration and payer acceptance. In the meantime, STAR-0310's entry into the atopic dermatitis market is a riskier but potentially valuable secondary asset that will need to differentiate itself to establish itself in a competitive space.

Astria is unique among clinical-stage biotechs in having a healthy cash position versus its burn rate that permits execution on both programs with no immediate dilution risk. That means this financial runway is available through mid-2027 - a luxury few small-cap biotechs can afford in order to fund pivotal trials. But the company still values itself on the basis of clinical execution - and investors will want to see whether the Phase 3 trial maintains efficacy and attack-free rates from earlier studies. STAR-0310 is still far from being validated, and the OX40 inhibition approach will need to establish a distinct advantage over existing therapies to gain traction.

data.title: Wise: Expanding Market Coverage And Digital Transformation To Drive Its Success
data.description:
Two decades ago, we had to wait at least a day or even go to a remittance center to claim the money our loved ones or clients sent. But today, money transfers can be completed instantly via personal computers and/or mobile devices. As the digital revolution peaks, wire transfers, e-wallets, and digital banks see newer opportunities to gain more popularity across the world.

One of the leading figures is Wise plc (OTCPK:WPLCF). With competitive pricing and a high concentration on cross-border money transfers, Wise keeps driving more demand for its solutions. Its topline growth and excess liquidity allow it to sustain its business operations and increase its international market presence. In addition, the continued freelancing and e-commerce boom offer more catalysts for growth.

Lastly, the stock is still a bit underpriced with some upside potential. This is supported by technical indicators.

Q3/9M25: When Lower Pricing Drove Volume

Since its inception, Wise plc has capitalized on its competitive fees to attract new customers. Over the years, its strategies have paid off. Its continued success is reflected in its most recent report.

During Q325, Wise generated a total underlying income or operating revenue reached £349.5M, up by 13% YoY from £307.9M, mainly driven by its card and other revenue. For nine months, the cumulative value reached £1.0B. This was a 17% YoY increase from £863.2 in 9M24.

Cross-border revenue remained its crown jewel as it comprised 60% of the total. It amounted to £212.9M, an increase of 3.2% YoY and 2.4% QoQ. Meanwhile, its underlying interest income grew by 24% from £31.3M to £38.8M. Lastly, its card and other revenue comprised 29% of the total revenue. It rose by 39.0% or £27.4M from £70.4M to £97.8M. What’s interesting is that all revenue components have consistently risen in the past eight quarters, suggesting sustained volume growth.

One of the key factors driving its growth is the increasing international market penetration. From the UK and most parts of Europe, Wise slowly made its way to the Americas and APAC. Today, it caters to 80 countries with over 9M active customers. This continues to drive its transaction volume growth. In Q325, it logged £37.8B in cross-border transactions. This is equivalent to £4,178.2 per customer and much higher than in Q324 and Q225 at £4,073.7 and £3,958.6. This also shows that every additional customer gave an extra £104.5 YoY. So as Wise penetrated more markets, it saw more money coming into the business.

This year, I believe it can sustain its growth with its increasing popularity in Southeast Asia. Note that it just launched its sixth live direct connection to a payment system in the Philippines. We have a lot of freelancers in the country, particularly on Upwork. As of FY23, the PH contributed the third-biggest share of freelancers on Upwork. We generated $45.9M, 14.9% higher YoY. What does it mean for Wise? More freelancing jobs from foreign countries, especially emerging ones, mean more cross-border transactions.

What Can Sustain Its Expansion

These are the things I considered before taking a position in this stock.

Liquidity

I think keeping high liquidity levels is easy for this business model. It does not have to spend a lot on infrastructure to expand unlike hotels, logistics, and telecom companies. Since it basically focuses largely on money transfers, Wise is not capital-intensive. It is a tech-driven company focusing on automation with lower overhead costs and limited physical presence like stores and ATMs. Hence, it does not have to borrow a lot of money or issue a lot of shares should it decide to expand more.

In addition, it has huge cash balances of £1.1B or 20% of the total assets. I excluded the amount related to customer funds for accuracy. It also has £4.0B in short-term financial investments, which are purely bonds. So, its financial assets are well-positioned for changing market conditions. If we add them, they can cover all liabilities and share capital.

I would also like to highlight why I believe having high cash and bond investments is strategic for Wise. First, currency volatility can pose risks since users can hold and send money in multiple currencies at once. Second, Wise should quickly get the required currencies for senders/recipients without delays, although it doesn’t keep large foreign currency reserves. This is why I think currency fluctuations can indirectly affect Wise. Third, it must ensure it can transfer and convert large volumes of money immediately. Remember that Wise capitalizes on volume rather than pricing. Hence, they can serve as buffers as the number of customers and cross-border transactions increase.

Competitive Advantage

Wise is known for its competitive pricing to attract volume. True enough, we see an uninterrupted rise in the number of active customers in the past nine quarters. From Q323 to Q325, it has already increased by 57%. Relative to its known peers, Wise has the most stable topline growth. It enjoys double-digit growth, which is logical since it has yet to capture more customers in other regions. The numbers are based on September 30, 2024 since Remitly (RELY) has not given any updates yet.

In addition, its margins are much better than PayPal (PYPL) and Remitly. It also has a decreasing cost sensitivity, with an operating leverage of only 29% versus 35% YoY. This shows its increased stability despite imposing lower fees amid market volatility. The pictures below show how much you will receive, whether via Wise or Remitly. That’s a difference of $0.37 in a single transaction.

More Freelancers, More Wire Transfers

Another potential growth catalyst is the continued boom of freelancing across the world. Over the past decade, websites like Upwork (UPWK) and Fiverr (FVRR) allowed many people to become self-employed or have a side hustle after office hours. But it was during the Covid-19 pandemic that freelancing peaked. Now that many businesses are implementing RTO policies, many employees are looking for remote work, either by freelancing or finding companies that offer these perks. A new study last December 2024 revealed that 58% of employers prefer a remote work setup, while 39% want a hybrid model. Only 3% want to work on-site.

Another study shows that there are about 1.5B freelancers in the world today. This comprises 46.7% of the global labor force. In the US alone, over 60M people are freelancers.

As the digital revolution persists, the number of freelancers may increase. By FY28, the number may reach 90M in the US. In addition, the global market value is expected to reach $7.4B in FY24 to $13.9B in FY28 with a CAGR of 16.8%. The expansion may continue as the internet becomes more accessible even in remote areas.

As a result, there may be an influx of cross-border transactions from employers to freelancers. This can also lead to higher cross-border revenues for money transfer service providers like Wise.

Price Ratios

Now, we will check how much the stock has changed its value since its IPO. To do this, we can use the P/E, P/CPS, P/S, and P/B Ratios.

The P/E, P/CPS, P/S, and P/B have a TP of $26.53, $16.54, $17.90 and $17.25, respectively. All price ratios show the stock is relatively cheaper than the average price from FY21 to FY24. With its current EPS, Cash Per Share, Sales Per Share, and Book Value Per Share, the stock must increase by at least 24% to become fairly valued. Due to the proximity of the results of the three price ratios, I think it is fair to assume that the price should be within $16-18.

DCF Model

I also used the DCF Model to assess Wise plc's intrinsic value, despite it not being capital-intensive. But because I believe its business model requires adequate buffers to cover instant transactions and currency fluctuation risks, this can give a precise valuation of Wise. The TP of $18.92 also shows the stock is very cheap right now. This indicates an upside potential of 41%. In addition, the DCF TP is close to the TP of three price ratios.

As you can see, I set the perpetual growth rate at 2.50%, which is much higher than my typical estimate of 2.00%. This is because I think Wise has a lot of potential to reach more individuals and businesses. The market is very far from being saturated. So, there is enough room for further expansion. Anyway, I also added a 2.00% rate to my sensitivity table to show that it is still underpriced at a lower perpetual growth rate. The difference in rates is 0.50%, which is quite high due to market volatility considerations. Meanwhile, I estimated WACC using the capital asset pricing model or CAPM. Lastly, I estimated FCFF and set the sum of the projected value in present value to proceed with the DCF valuation.

Technicals

WPLCF shows a slightly weaker momentum after its recent pullback. At $13.40, the stock is trading about 8% lower than its resistance. But we see bulls trying to push the price up again. Last February 12, they rejected any selling price lower than $13.12. Instead, this became a newfound support for some to enter/reenter WPLCF. As you can see, bears tried to pull the price lower to $13.15 last Friday, but bullish rejections continued. It just stayed within $13.40-13.50 as buying volume offset selling pressures.

Technical indicators also suggest a weaker momentum today. MACD continues to decrease after crossing below the signal line. They may drop to the negative region, which can confirm the downtrend. In addition, the histogram remains negative. Meanwhile, the RSI shows there’s a lot of room for a rebound as it remains below the overbought region.

Amid all this, I think the recent trend is logical. First, the stock hit the 0.786 Fibonacci retracement when it opened last February 12. It was also where the bullish rejection happened. This could explain why we saw higher prices in the next two days. We also saw bears trying to drag the price down to this point again last February 13 and 14 before rebounding again. WPLCF may retest its resistance again soon and hit our take profit point at $14.73.

If it fails to break the resistance again, it may retrace to the 0.618 point. This appears logical since it is very near our stop loss point at $12.07 using the 1:1 Reward-Risk Ratio. Hitting either of the two can confirm a bearish reversal.

Conclusion

Wise plc is a solid company with increasing market penetration, competitiveness, and liquidity. These things can help support its expansion as the digital revolution continues. With many emerging economies adapting to the digital trend, Wise can see more opportunities for its business. In addition, the stock is underpriced and may set new highs after recent retracements. I am setting a buy rating for this stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Weitz Ultra Short Government Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
The Ultra Short Government Fund returned +1.09% in the fourth quarter compared to a +1.13% return for the ICE BofAML U.S. 6-Month Treasury Bill Index (6-Month Treasury). For the full calendar year, the Fund returned +4.87% compared to a +5.35% return for the index.

Overview

While longer-term interest rates continued to move higher in the fourth quarter, surprisingly, short-term interest rates moved lower. The Federal Open Market Committee of the Federal Reserve cut (or lowered) the Federal Funds Rate twice during the last quarter of the year after cutting for the first time in mid-September. The lower bound of the Federal Funds Rate declined 50 basis points (one half of one percent) in the fourth quarter, ending the year at 4.25%. As a result, shorter-term U.S. Treasury security yields were priced lower during the quarter.

The graph below highlights the path of yields for 3- and 6-month Treasury Bills during the quarter, which are a reasonable proxy for shorter-term investments that would be suitable for the Ultra Short Government Fund.

As we have repeatedly mentioned in the past, the Fed’s monetary policy decisions (e.g., changes in short-term interest rates) will continue to affect all investments within our opportunity set. As a result, our yield and return will invariably follow the path dictated by the Fed’s monetary policy, as we frequently reinvest maturities with holdings that mature in a short period of time. As of December 31, 2024, 45.1% of our portfolio was invested in U.S. Treasury notes, 0.1% in investment-grade asset-backed securities, and 54.8% in a high-quality money market fund and cash equivalents. The average effective duration as of December 31, 2024 was 0.2 years, down from 0.3 years at September 30, 2024. The Fund’s 30-day yield declined to 4.31% as of December 31, 2024, down from 4.51% as of September 30, 2024.

Under normal market conditions, the Fund will invest at least 80% of its net assets in obligations issued or guaranteed by the U.S. government and its government-related entities. The balance of Fund assets may be invested in U.S. investment-grade debt securities. Additionally, the Fund will maintain an average effective duration of one year or less. Duration is a measure of how sensitive the portfolio may be to changes in interest rates. All else being equal, a lower-duration bond portfolio is less sensitive to changes in interest rates than a bond portfolio with a higher duration.

Over time, this shorter-term focus (duration of less than one year) is intended to generate higher total returns than cash or money market funds, while also taking less interest rate risk than a bond portfolio with a higher duration. The Fund’s principal investment strategies and objectives of providing current income, protecting principal, and providing liquidity remain our primary goals.

We think the investing landscape for allocation investors is well-balanced. In our view, the Fund's securities offer adequate long-term capital appreciation potential. High-quality bonds yielding well above 4% have enhanced the current income outlook. And sizeable holdings of short-maturity Treasury securities and cash provide healthy ballast with respectable yields. As always, we encourage investors to evaluate the strategy on a total-return basis over longer time horizons.

data.title: YETI Holdings: Initiating A 'Buy' With Strong Growth Prospects
data.description:
YETI Holdings Inc. (NYSE:YETI) is a well-known premium brand for outdoor living, recognized for its high-performance and durable products such as drinkware, coolers, and outdoor gears. Its consumers are mainly outdoor enthusiasts those like recreational camping, climbing, hiking, fishing, hunting, and traveling. I think YETI has been effectively executing growth strategies through product enrichment and international expansion, positioning the company for long-term growth. I am initiating a “BUY” rating on YETI with a one-year target price of $52 as per my estimation.

Growth Drivers

In my opinion, YETI has two main growth drivers:

1. Product enrichment.

Drinkware has been contributing most of YETI’s total revenue, and YETI has been expanding its product offerings since 2017. Not only numbers of drinkware collections, but also a wider variety of sizes and colors. In FY23 it introduced to the market wineware like beverage bucket and cocktail shaker. In FY24 it extended wineware line to include shot glasses and flask, and YETI went even further to now offering food storage and cast-iron skillets. This is a great way of increasing per-customer spending and can also attract new customers from adjacent product categories.

2. International Expansion.

YETI’s international business is growing at a significantly faster pace than its US business. While US revenue has been growing at a single-digit rate, international revenue is growing at over 30% year over year. The contribution of international revenue to total revenue increased quickly from around 12% back in FY2020 to now close to 19% in FY2024.

Yet, its international expansion still has lots of room for growth potential when comparing to other premium consumer brands. YETI is ramping up in the Japanese market in 2025, and is focusing on Direct-to-Consumer [DTC] growth in all of its international markets. I think it is reasonable to anticipate that YETI’s international business can reach to 40-50% range of total revenue in the next decade.

Recent Result

On February 13th, YETI released its Q4-FY24 results. I have a few key takeaways from its earnings. First, I think YETI’s operating results are very strong. YETI reported 9% overall revenue growth, 59% gross margin, and adjustment operating income of $309m - a 17% growth vs prior year.

Secondly, I think YETI’s cash management is very robust. Operating income to cash flow through is very strong, debt level continues to decrease. Capital allocation is focused on technology enhancements, new product development and supply chain improvements – all areas that could directly boost topline and generate operation efficiencies. On top of that, the $200m share repurchase program got accelerated in 2024, and further increased to $450m – a great way to return value to shareholders and showing strong confidence from YETI’s management. EPS increased 21% due to strong performance, prudent cash management and share repurchase all combined.

Outlook and Valuation

Looking forward, due to the nature of YETI’s business, I do not expect any fast-paced growth at all, but an overall steady 6-7% yoy growth is very realistic and reasonable. I anticipate mid-single digit growth for YETI’s US business. 3% growth from volume due to new products, and 2% from moderate price increases, makes it 5% yoy growth going forward. For its international business, I expect 15% yoy growth starting FY25 and will slow down to 10% in the medium term, which means in 10 years' time, YETI’s international business will take about 45% of its global revenue. With that, YETI’s revenue is growing at 6.8% CAGR for the next 10 years.

On profitability, I expect slight cost improvement and margin expansion. I expect 20 basis points yoy cost improvement as well as 20 basis points yoy reduction on SG&A expenses mainly driven by operational efficiencies. With that, gross margin will be flattish trending to 60%, operating margin will be picking up gradually from 13.4% to 17%.

The WACC is calculated to be 10.8% assuming: risk-free rate 3.6%; beta 1.07; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 4.25%; equity $3.03 billion; debt $0.17 billion; tax rate 24%. The DCF is summarized as follows:

Discounting all the future FCF, the fair value is calculated to be $52 per share, as per my estimates.

Potential Risks

  • Competition. Among all its competitors, YETI provides the widest range of products and top qualities, but still facing intense competition, especially in the drinkware market. YETI is competing directly with several other premium brands, such as ORCA (Outdoor Recreation Company of America) and Pelican Products. RTIC, Hydro Flask, and Stanley products are less expensive but also less durable. Igloo Coolers, although lack of premium branding and quality, target the budget segment and mass market and could erode YETI’s market share.
  • Global Macro and Consumer Spending. Consumers could be sensitive to premium price, especially during economic downturns. They could be select a lower tier product for budget consideration. And this would mean revenue reductions for YETI.
  • Tariff Exposure. US imposing further tariffs on China would be a risk factor, negatively impacting sales and profit. The positive side is that the company has already experienced the challenge back in 2018-2019 tariff periods, and has proactively further diversified its supply chain in FY24. In the Q4 earning call, Management shared that the goal is to have approximately 20% of global drinkware capacity outside China by the end of 2024 and 50% by the end of 2025. Management also mentioned that they could potentially increase price to offset tariffs impact. Thus, I think YETI’s tariff exposure is manageable.
  • Foreign Exchange. With YETI’s international expansion is underway, the continuously strong US dollars could negatively impact its reported revenue and earnings.

Conclusion

In my opinion, YETI has found a winning growth strategy –focusing on product enrichment as well as international expansion. It is also very well executing this strategy towards future long-term value accretion. As a result, I initiate a “BUY” rating to YETI with a one-year target price of $52.

data.title: Weitz Short Duration Income Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
The Short Duration Income Fund’s Institutional Class returned +0.51% in the fourth quarter compared to a -0.02% return for the Bloomberg 1-3 Year U.S. Aggregate Index, bringing calendar year returns to +5.70% and +4.39%, respectively. This marks the sixth consecutive calendar year that the Fund has outperformed this benchmark. Long-term performance certainly matters most but we are particularly pleased with how well the Fund performed in the fourth quarter compared to the index and the Morningstar US Fund Short-Term Bond category (-0.10%) given the rise in interest rates during the quarter.

Market Overview

Another year in the proverbial books. Looking back on 2024, there have been quite a few surprises.

The U.S. economy remained resilient despite “experts” calling for an imminent recession. Stock indexes registered new highs as AI (artificial intelligence) fever seemed to promise endless prosperity. Arguably the greatest political comeback in history occurred on November 5th. Cryptocurrencies surged. Corporate credit spreads reached all-time lows (i.e., higher prices) – more on that later. Even the 79th number in the periodic table (gold), that “barbarous relic” per John Maynard Keynes, reached all-time highs.

If 2024 were a gum flavor, it might be called Bubblelicious. It was a year where everyone seemingly went to Vegas and came home a winner. But for fixed income investors, one of the biggest surprises could be what occurred in the U.S. Treasury bond market. It was the tale of two time periods: the time before the Federal Reserve (Fed) decisively cut short-term interest rates on September 18—and the time after.

Leading up to September 18, Treasury interest rates declined across the yield curve by more than one-half of a percent in 2- and 3-year Treasuries. But after September 18, and despite two additional short-term interest rate cuts by the Fed, interest rates increased meaningfully across the yield curve.

For example, 10-year Treasuries increased nearly a full percent (87 basis points) from the Fed’s first cut on September 18 through year-end.

Why is this a surprise? As the graph to the right illustrates (using the 10-year Treasury), it is not common for bond yields to increase—particularly so markedly—following the start of a short-term interest-rate easing (lowering) cycle.

According to Jim Bianco of Bianco Research, the current easing cycle, as measured by the market reaction in 10-year bond yields 100 days after the first cut, has recorded the second-largest increase among the 12 Fed rate-cutting campaigns since 1963, with only the 1981 cycle experiencing a greater surge. Threats of tariffs and their potential inflationary effects from the incoming administration, economic resilience, and uncertainty around fiscal policy are often cited as reasons for the atypical move in interest rates amid a rate cutting cycle. It could also reflect the bond market reacting to a Fed that, after infamously deeming inflation “transitory” a few years ago, may now be prematurely claiming victory over inflation.

The chart below provides a view of rates over time across the yield curve. The chart includes yield curve rates on the day before the Fed’s market-moving cut on September 18 to illustrate movement in rates from the cut through year-end. A broad gauge of the U.S. Treasury market managed to post a small gain in 2024, thanks to coupon income from higher base rates. However, 2024 marked the fourth straight year of rising yields for the benchmark 10-year Treasury, the longest stretch since 1981.

Portfolio Positioning

The table below shows the change in allocation to various sectors, from the prior quarter and from the prior year. This summary provides a view over time of how we have allocated capital. Since our goal is to invest in sectors that we believe offer the best risk-adjusted returns, our allocations may change significantly over time.

The most notable observation regarding the above is the year-over-year additions to mortgage-backed securities (MBS), both agency and non-agency MBS. The nearly 17% increase in MBS resulted primarily from reductions in corporate bonds, Asset-Backed Securities (ABS), and U.S. Treasuries. MBS (agency and non-agency) continue to represent reasonable opportunities to deploy capital in shorter-duration assets, especially compared to corporate credit. Investments in 2024 principally represented CMOs (Collateralized Mortgage Obligations) in both agency and non-agency MBS. Our investments came with structural protections/seniority regarding principal prepayment, which typically lead to shorter average lives for the investments.

Investment activity was strong in the fourth quarter with more than $190 million of new securities added to the Fund. This exceeded the Fund’s quarterly paydowns and maturities of securities (approximately $90 million in the fourth quarter). By design, the Fund has a distinct feature of having about 25%-30% of its holdings paydown or mature in any given year. This allows for frequent reinvestment of investor capital into areas of the fixed-income market that we believe provide the best current relative value opportunities.

Noteworthy additions included:

Mortgage Backed Securities highlighted above.

Asset-backed securities (ABS) home improvement receivables issued by Greensky Home Improvement (GSKY) and automobile receivables issued LendBuzz (LBZST). In keeping with most of the Fund’s historical ABS investments, these fourth quarter investments are short average life (less than 2 years), senior securities from recent securitizations. Treasury securities in the 3-year area as interest rates rose over three quarters of one percent in 4Q across most of the yield curve.

Top Quarterly Contributors

Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs), Asset-Backed Securities (principally auto ABS), and Collateralized Mortgage Obligations (CMOs)

Top Quarterly Detractors

U.S. Treasury securities and non-agency Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities (CMBS).

Fund Strategy Revisited

Our approach consists primarily of investing in a diversified portfolio of high-quality bonds while maintaining an overall portfolio average duration of 1.0 to 3.5 years. We may invest up to 15% in fixed-income securities that are not considered investment-grade (such as high-yield and convertible bonds as well as preferred and convertible preferred stock), and we do so when we perceive the risk/ reward characteristics to be favorable.

We do not, and will not, try to mimic any index as we construct our portfolio. We believe our flexible mandate is a differentiator that allows us to navigate any environment – and utilize a bottom-up, research-driven approach and select portfolio assets one security at a time based on our view of opportunities in the marketplace.

Overall, we strive to be adequately compensated for the risks assumed while seeking to maximize investment (or reinvestment) income and avoid interest-rate bets, particularly those that depend on interest rates going down.

Maintaining a liquid (e.g. Treasuries and agency MBS) and overall, up-in-quality portfolio positioning that we believe can generate higher income returns than respective indexes position us well as we begin a new year. 2025 is a year that could get messy in so many unpredictable ways. But also, it may be one that will allow us to lean into and be liquidity providers if/when more favorable risk/reward opportunities present themselves.

We are thankful for the trust and partnership many have placed in our firm and its investment process. 2025: a New Year, a new blank page, and a renewed commitment to keep earning our investment partners’ trust.

data.title: SG Holdings: Still No Compelling Investment
data.description:
In October 2024, I initiated coverage for SG Holdings (OTCPK:SGHDY) (OTCPK:SGHHF) with a hold rating. The SGHDY ticker has remained flat, and that is due to the low volume. The listing in Japan (9143:JP) actually lost 6% of its value, which translated to US dollars would a 5.1% loss. The company recently announced its third quarter and nine months ended earnings for the year ending March 2025 and in this report I will be analyzing the earnings and updating my price target for the stock.

If you are not familiar with SG Holdings, you can read the description of the company that I provided in an earlier report.

SG Holdings: A Business Focused On Door-to-Door Delivery

SG Holdings is a Japanese company that operates through three segments, namely Delivery Business, Logistics Business, and Real Estate Business. During the year ending in March 2024, the company had net sales of ¥1,317 billion or $8.6 billion with net income of ¥58.3 billion or $380 million. It shows that while SG Holdings is likely not a familiar name to many, it is a company with billions of dollars in sales, sporting a market cap of over $6 billion.

The delivery business consists of traditional door-to-door deliveries but also increases transport services for corporate clients and vehicle management, freight loading and unloading, security, and cleaning services for large-scale facilities. The logistics business provides freight forwarding solutions and 3PL (third-party logistics) solutions. The real estate business includes the company's warehouses, which derive sales from leases.

Generally, driven by ecommerce, the trend for parcel volumes is directed upwards. However, at times there are also soft spots in the market and this year seems to be such a year with January parcel deliveries down 2.3% and year-to-date volumes are down almost 4%.

Expolanka Continues To Drive Sales Growth For SG Holdings

During the nine months ended, sales grew by 12.4% to ¥1.12 billion yen. The delivery business saw revenues decline by 0.6% to ¥779.7 million yen, driven by lower volumes partially offset by higher transportation management sales and higher unit prices per parcel. The logistics business saw a 73% increase in sales to ¥282.8 million due to higher cargo volumes and freight rates as Expolanka as well as the inclusion of Chilled & Frozen Logistics into the consolidated scope of the company. Excluding the addition of C&F, the sales would have been 54% higher. The real estate business saw sales growing 218.4% to ¥17.8 million, driven by the sale of real estate.

While revenues increased more than 12%, the operating income grew 7% this was driven by growth of the logistics business also increasing outsourcing expenses while depreciation and amortization, as well as other expenses, increased partially offset by lower personnel expenses. The consolidation of C&F also had a modest contribution to earnings after amortization of goodwill (purchasing price allocation).

SG Holdings Does Stock Is Still A Hold

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

While SG Holdings has upside base on the company median EV/EBITDA and well as the peer group EV/EBITDA, to me, the investment case remains uncompelling. The reason is that the company's fundamental support for higher stock prices has declined since 2023 and while I do see 19% upside towards the price target for FY25, in the years after the upside is in the mid-single-digit range which is not enough to score a buy rating and I do believe that the company will largely depend on debt fueled M&A to grow the business at a higher rate.

Conclusion: SG Holdings Too Many Uncertainties

What holds for freight and logistics companies is that those companies had to rationalize after the golden times seen during the pandemic. With those times behind us and the global uncertainty on trade increasing on top of rising costs on labor and materials. I don't view the investment case for SG Holdings a compelling one. The stock is undervalued, but it seems to me that the expectations of debt fueled M&A is the primary reason for that, with little upside to the stock price beyond FY25. As a result, I am maintaining my hold rating.

data.title: Carvana Q4 Preview: Used Car Sales Are Booming
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig

Investment Thesis

Carvana (NYSE:CVNA) shares are up more than 50% since the last time I wrote on them in mid-January. A big driver behind this major increase has been the market shaking off the Hindenburg short report, which is something that, I thought, had little long run impact during my last research.

Now, just as I have voiced in each of my last two pieces of research, used car sales are turning up and to the right. This is obviously encouraging for Carvana, given that’s what the whole business is based on.

As more people return to office following the COVID-19 pandemic, it’s forcing people to finally upgrade their vehicles after years of making older cars last longer. With rising new car prices, more people are looking at used car sales. This is an incredible catalyst for Carvana.

Heading into earnings, I continue to believe shares are a strong buy.

Why I’m Doing Follow Up Coverage

During my last piece of research, I explained how I thought Carvana had set a great foundation for 2025. Having renewed their key credit facility with Ally Bank, along with improving EPS numbers, have made me optimistic on the future of the company. I argued that they had more than 65% upside going forward.

Adding to this, the other key piece of the puzzle that’s been fairly solved for Carvana is the loan piece. Default rates have dropped. An auto loan default occurs if a customer’s payment is 30 or more days late. When this happens, customers may see a hit to their credit score or a repossession of the vehicle.

These default rates have plateaued, which is huge for underwriting auto loans (which is key to their business). According to Cox Auto, as of November, default rates were up 15.3% year-over-year as compared to November 2023, but the month-over-month rates were down 7.7% from October to November. Rates are plateauing, which is good for Carvana.

With this, the purpose of me doing follow-up coverage heading into earnings is to show the strong (and increasing) tail winds that Carvana has for 2025.

Why Used Car Sales Are Booming

Cox Auto reported on February 14th bullish used car sales which has me excited about the industry and Carvana in 2025. According to Cox Auto, there were 1.41 million used cars sold at retail during January, which was up 8% year-over-year.

The research also shows a forecast of 20.1 million retail used-vehicle sales in 2025, which, if met, would be a 1.2% increase as compared to 2024. To me, this 1.2% increase feels conservative given the strong start in January.

As Cox Auto and Auto Live Market view shows in the chart below, in the first full month of 2025, used-vehicle retail sales are already better than the first full month of each of the last three years. January 2025’s mark appears to be about 100,000 more than both 2024 and 2022.

However, it’s clear that the numbers fluctuate greatly. 2023 started off higher than both 2022 and 2024 but then finished below both years, I don’t think 2025 will follow the same path. It’s encouraging that 2025 has gotten off to a good start.

As I’ve mentioned already a few times in this article, I believe a big reason why sales are up is because of the fact that so many Americans are finally returning to in-person work following the COVID-19 pandemic.

There appears to be a cultural shift among employers to return to the office. President Trump signed an executive order entitled “return to in-person work,” for Federal employees. I think this trend is already in motion in the private sector (and now accelerating).

This is a huge tailwind Carvana. According to an article published by Forbes, full-time office workers spend an average of $1,020 per month on commuting expenses, which is more than double what hybrid workers pay. Carvana can grab a large chunk of this through used car sales.

Of course, a large majority of this monthly expense is things like gas and car repairs, but in order to maintain an in-office job, people need reliable transportation. Carvana should benefit.

As we see a steady uptick in people returning to the office, I think sales will continue to accelerate. People who have been working from home, perhaps with an unreliable (but tolerable) vehicle, will need to potentially buy a used car in order to get to work.

Q4 Preview

Carvana is set to report its Q4 EPS and revenue on Wednesday, February 19th after market close. On the earnings-per-share front, eight analysts project Carvana will post a total of $0.32. Seeking Alpha doesn't provide a year-over-year growth forecast for this projection because EPS was negative this time last year. So, I would say this would be pretty solid.

However, it’s interesting to note as we approach two days until earnings, that Carvana’s EPS projections wide apart. The same eight analysts estimate this EPS number to fall anywhere in between $0.07 and $0.74/share. I think this means there is room for shares to beat to the upside.

On the revenue front, Carvana should do well. According to 19 analysts, Carvana is estimated to post a Q4 revenue total of $3.34 billion. If this number is met, this would represent strong 37.94% year-over-year growth.

Carvana’s revisions over the past three months also are favorable. In the last three months, there have been three EPS revisions, with two of them being upward and one of them being downward. Similarly, out of 10 revenue revisions, seven have been upward, and only three have been downward. I think this shows most analysts are becoming increasingly optimistic about Carvana as we head toward earnings.

Valuation

In my last two pieces of research, I’ve really honed in on Carvana’s Non-GAAP P/E ratio. Each of the last two times I’ve written on Carvana, their Non-GAAP price-to-earnings ratio has been extremely higher than the sector median.

This research is no different. In fact, Carvana’s forward Non-GAAP P/E ratio is at an even higher premium to the sector median than it was in either of my two previous pieces of research. As of right now, Carvana has a forward Non-GAAP P/E of 192.63. This is a 1,024.99% premium to the sector median, which is just 17.12.

Seeking Alpha Quant grades Carvana as an F on this metric. Once again, this is not new information for investors who follow the used car giant.

But as I mentioned last time, I believe Carvana’s revenue growth more than makes up for this deficiency. Carvana has a forward revenue growth rate of 5.42%, which is 77.18% higher than the sector median of 3.06%. Seeking Alpha Quant gives Carvana a grade of a B on this metric.

As I argued last time, I still believe that Carvana should be trading at a 3x price-to-sales ratio. Their bottom line growth is still impressive (hence the expected 37.94% YoY expected bottom line growth in Q4), and I believe that overall growth will continue to excel as more people return to their office.

Right now, Carvana has a forward price-to-sales ratio of 2.72. If the company has a price-to-sales ratio that converges on the rate of 3x like I think they should, this would represent about 10.2% upside in shares.

Risks

I think the biggest risk for Carvana now is that used auto sales slowdown. As we saw from the chart earlier, we’ve seen a couple examples in recent years where growth starts strong but then fizzles out. In 2022 and 2023 especially, used car sales started out well but then tailed off near the end of the year.

I don’t think this will happen for Carvana. For me, a good majority of my belief hinges on return to office mandates like Trump’s executive order, which I believe will cause used car sales to rise. I think this will be enough to break the trend.

Helping to hedge this, Carvana has a very strong inventory turnover ratio, which means the used auto retailer can adjust their inventory needs to customer expectations if the return to office trend changes. According to Seeking Alpha, Carvana’s asset turnover ratio is currently at 1.74, which is significantly higher than the sector median.

I think these two things will continue to be tailwinds for the company.

Bottom Line

I think Carvana is set up for a strong 2025 and a strong Q4 report later this week. With Trump’s return to office executive order, I think used car dealers (like Carvana) have undeniable tailwinds. Used car sales are already off to a good start in 2025. In fact, other used car dealers like Penske Automotive have reported strong Q4’s (their revenue hit a record in 2024). I think the writing is on the wall for a solid quarter for Carvana.

With this, shares have more room to run. That’s why I continue to believe shares are a strong buy going into Wednesday’s earnings print.

data.title: Weitz Core Plus Income Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
The Core Plus Income Fund’s Institutional Class returned -2.30% for the fourth quarter compared to a -3.06% return for the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (Agg), bringing calendar year returns to +3.37% and +1.25% respectively. Longer-term results (3-, 5-, and 10-year) continue to outpace the index (see performance table on page 4). The Fund’s outperformance for the year was driven primarily by sector and security selection, led by collateralized loan obligations (CLOs), asset-backed securities (ABS) and corporate bonds.

Overview

While there were many surprises in 2024, one of the biggest could be what occurred in the U.S. Treasury bond market. It was the tale of two time periods: the time before the Federal Reserve (Fed) decisively cut short-term interest rates on September 18—and the time after. Leading up to September 18, Treasury interest rates declined across the yield curve by more than one-half of a percent in 2- and 3-year Treasuries. But after September 18, and despite two additional short-term interest rate cuts by the Fed, interest rates increased meaningfully across the yield curve. For example, 10-year Treasuries increased nearly a full percent (87 basis points) from the Fed’s first cut on September 18 through year-end.

Why is this a surprise? As the graph below illustrates (using the 10-year Treasury), it is not common for bond yields to increase—particularly so markedly—following the start of a short-term interest-rate easing (lowering) cycle.

According to Jim Bianco of Bianco Research, the current easing cycle, as measured by the market reaction in 10-year bond yields 100 days after the first cut, has recorded the second-largest increase among the 12 Fed rate-cutting campaigns since 1963, with only the 1981 cycle experiencing a greater surge. Threats of tariffs and their potential inflationary effects from the incoming administration, economic resilience, and uncertainty around fiscal policy are often cited as reasons for the atypical move in interest rates amid a rate cutting cycle. It could also reflect the bond market reacting to a Fed that, after infamously deeming inflation “transitory” a few years ago, may now be prematurely claiming victory over inflation.

Portfolio Positioning

The table on the next page shows the change in allocation to various sectors, from the prior quarter and from the prior year. This summary provides a view over time of how we have allocated capital. Since our goal is to invest in sectors that we believe offer the best risk-adjusted returns, our allocations may change significantly over time.

The most notable increase in sector allocation during the fourth quarter (and for the calendar year) was an increase in mortgage-backed securities (MBS). Prior to 2023, the Fund had not had a meaningful investment in MBS. Since that time, we have steadily increased our allocation to over 30%. Our focus has been and remains higher coupon agency MBS, with coupons of 4.50%-6.50%. Agency MBS is an area within benchmark categories that has rarely looked more attractive relative to investment grade corporate bonds. Low investment grade spreads can make a lot of alternatives look good but the high-quality nature of agency MBS and what we believe is a better return profile relative to investment grade corporate bonds remain a positive investment theme for our investment portfolios.

In addition to MBS, we added Treasury securities, principally in 10–20-year maturities. We also added a wide variety of securitized investments, including investments within Auto ABS, CLOs, CMBS, and whole business.

In terms of overall portfolio metrics, the Fund’s average effective maturity increased to 9.5 years as of December 31, 2024, from 9.0 years as of September 30, 2024, while our average effective duration increased to 5.8 years from 5.5 years over the same period, compared to the Agg’s average effective duration of 6.0 years on December 31. These measures provide a guide to the Fund’s interest rate sensitivity. A higher average effective duration increases the Fund’s price sensitivity to changes in interest rates (either up or down).

As of December 31, our high-yield exposure as a percentage of net assets stayed at 3.9%, similarly to September 30, 2024. The Fund can invest up to 25% of net assets in high yield, therefore we have ample capacity to take advantage of valuation discrepancies/ opportunities in the high yield area.

The Fund’s yield to worst (YTW) remains higher than the Bloomberg Barclays U.S. Aggregate index, while the Fund’s interest rate risk (duration) continues to be lower. Constructing a portfolio that produces “carry” – income achieved from regular coupon payments – (via the YTW measurement) and lower interest rate sensitivity (via lower duration) is a feature of our flexible mandate and ability to invest in other higher quality securities that reside outside of the benchmark. The Fund’s 30-day SEC yield was 5.18% (subsidized) and 5.14% (unsubsidized) as of December 31, 2024 (subsidized yield reflects fee waivers and/or expense reimbursements, while unsubsidized yield does not).

Top Quarterly Contributors

Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs): CLO were the top contributor to performance due to strong coupon income and modest price appreciation due to tighter spreads

Asset Backed Securities: Our ABS portfolio was a positive contributor to performance due to high coupon income and price appreciation, as ABS spreads tightened throughout the year.

Top Quarterly Detractors

U.S. Treasury Bonds: U.S. Treasuries were the largest detractor to performance. With a duration of over 10 years, our Treasury portfolio experienced unrealized mark-to-market losses as interest rates rose significantly during the quarter.

Agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS): Agency MBS was the second-largest detractor to performance as interest rates rose and mortgage-backed spreads widened during the quarter.

Corporate Bonds: Corporate bonds were a slight detractor to performance as higher interest rates more than offset coupon income and modest spread tightening during the quarter.

Fund Strategy Revisited

Our approach consists primarily of investing in a diversified portfolio of high-quality bonds while maintaining an overall portfolio average duration of 3.5 to 7 years. We may seek to capture attractive coupon income and potential price appreciation by investing in longer-duration and lower-quality bonds when attractively priced. We may also invest up to 25% in fixed income securities that are not considered investment grade (such as high yield and convertible bonds as well as preferred and convertible preferred stock), and we do so when we perceive the risk/reward characteristics to be favorable.

We do not, and will not, try to mimic any particular index as we construct our portfolio. We believe our flexible mandate and high-conviction portfolio will benefit investors over the long term. We utilize a bottom-up, research-driven approach and select portfolio assets one security at a time based on our view of opportunities in the marketplace. Our fixed income research is not dependent on, but often benefits from, the due diligence work our equity teammates conduct on companies and industries.

Overall, we strive to be adequately compensated for the risks assumed to maximize investment (or reinvestment) yield and to avoid making interest rate bets, particularly those that depend on interest rates going down. We have often maintained a lower duration profile than the index, particularly in very low-yield environments. Our shorter duration profile has benefited shareholders in periods of rising interest rates.

Maintaining a liquid (e.g. Treasuries and agency MBS) and overall, up-in-quality portfolio positioning that we believe can generate higher income returns than respective indexes position us well as we begin a new year a year that could get messy in so many unpredictable ways. But also, it could be one that will particularly allow us to lean into and be liquidity providers if/when more favorable risk/reward opportunities present themselves.

We are thankful for the trust and partnership many have in our firm and its investment process. 2025: a New Year…a new blank page…. and renewed commitment to keep earning our investment partner’s trust.
