data.title: Richtech Robotics: Sell On Poor Results And Elevated Valuation
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Richtech Robotics Inc. or “Richtech” (NASDAQ:RR) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier article on the company.

At the beginning of the year, I initiated coverage of robotic solutions provider Richtech Robotics with a "Sell" rating due to an ugly combination of elevated business risks, persistent dilution and poor shareholder communication.

Since that time, the company has filed its annual report on form 10-K for FY2024 and its quarterly report on form 10-Q for Q1/FY2025.

As usual, Richtech did not issue an earnings press release and abstained from holding a conference call, thus leaving investors largely in the dark with regard to its financial performance.

However, taking a closer look at the company's financial results explains management's reluctance to communicate with investors on this topic:

While sales were up slightly on a year-over-year basis, revenues of just $1.3 million aren't exactly a reason to celebrate. However, the company's gross margin advanced to a new all-time high of 90.2% as the recent shift to a leasing model (Robotics-as-a-Service or "RaaS") has resulted in both revenues and related costs being recognized over the lease term.

While management has stated a number of reasons for the business model change, like creating recurring revenue streams, stronger customer relationships and reduced sales cycles, it is important to note that the Richtech is not a software company.

In fact, Richtech is trying to sell expensive robotic solutions with a focus on the hospitality and service industry.

Given the requirement for substantial upfront investments with unclear return profiles, customers have been hesitant to adopt the company's robotic solutions in recent years.

To address the issue, Richtech has not only decided to offer lease financing to potential customers, the company has also committed to operate 20 Walmart-located restaurants in Arizona, Colorado, and Texas under a franchise agreement with Ghost Kitchens America's "One Kitchen" brand.

According to the disclosures made in the 10-Q, the company has opened its first One Kitchen store in a Walmart location near Chicago last month.

One Kitchen offers a unique and interactive dining experience, showcasing the potential of our robotic technology to transform the food service industry. We anticipate opening additional One Kitchen stores near Atlanta and San Diego in late spring 2025.

Two weeks ago, Richtech also opened its first "Clouffee & Tea" store in Las Vegas:

Our first Clouffee & Tea store opened its doors in February 2025, marking our entry into the rapidly growing market for automated coffee and tea service. Clouffee & Tea combines our advanced robotics technology with a curated selection of premium beverages, creating a unique and engaging customer experience. We are confident in the potential of this brand and plan to open additional Clouffee & Tea locations in the coming months.

Last year, Richtech raised $19.4 million in net proceeds from a combined stock and warrant offering priced at $1.35 per share.

Following the meteoric rise of the company's shares over the past two months, Richtech has raised substantial, additional proceeds from warrant exercises.

However, common shareholder dilution wasn't limited to warrant exercises. During the quarter, the company issued an additional 4.1 million shares for unspecified "technology development services", 3.4 million shares to the "employee and director incentive pool" and 0.4 million shares to "advisors and consultants as compensation for services rendered".

As a result, outstanding shares have increased to 114.5 million.

Last week, Richtech agreed to issue 2.7 million new warrants with an exercise price of $4.00 to a fund managed by Armistice Capital in exchange for exercising 2.7 million existing warrants at a price of $1.35 for gross proceeds of $3.6 million.

The company also entered into a settlement agreement with AC Sunshine Securities LLC, pursuant to which Richtech agreed to pay $430,000.

Even when considering the settlement payment and assuming additional cash usage, I would estimate Richtech's current cash balance to be well above $40 million.

That said, the company's cash usage is likely to increase further due to a number of issues:

  • significantly increased investments in sales and marketing
  • requirement to provide lease financing to customers
  • material expansion of the company's One Kitchen and Clouffee & Tea store fleet

An assumed cash burn rate of $5 million per quarter would translate into approximately two years of runway for Richtech. Given this issue, I wouldn't be surprised to see the company returning to the capital markets in the not-too-distant-future.

For my part, I continue to dislike the elevated risks and substantial cash drain resulting from the recent change in the company's business model and decision to operate restaurants and coffee stores at scale.

Depending on the pace of additional store openings, Richtech's hospitality revenue will eclipse the company's core robotics business sooner rather than later, which is likely to negatively impact gross margins, particularly given the fact that Richtech has no experience in operating restaurants.

Even when generously assuming FY2025 sales to reach $10 million, the company is currently trading at a revenue multiple of more than 30x, which apparently doesn't make much sense.

In addition, with NVIDIA (NVDA) having recently disposed of its stake in Serve Robotics (SERV), the recent robotics hype might come to an end soon as momentum traders leave for assumed greener pastures:

Considering the ugly combination of elevated valuation, persistent dilution, poor investor communications and very risky business model, I am reiterating my "Sell" rating on Richtech Robotics' common shares.

Risks

A continuation of the recent robotics hype could result in momentum traders chasing the shares aggressively again.

Bottom Line

Richtech Robotics' investor communications continue to leave much to be desired, as evidenced by the fact that the company does neither issue quarterly earnings press releases nor hold conference calls.

However, a closer look at Richtech's recent SEC filings reveals a story of persistent dilution and material cash burn resulting from recent business model changes.

At this point, it is difficult to envision the company growing into its elevated valuation anytime soon if ever.

Consequently, I am reiterating my "Sell" rating on Richtech Robotics' common shares.

data.title: February's 5 Dividend Growth Stocks With Yields Up To 10.49%
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

For some background on this monthly publication, here is my view on dividend growth stocks:

Dividend growth stocks aren't always the most exciting investments out there. They often aren't grabbing the headlines, and they aren't the stocks running up hundreds of percentages in a year. In fact, they are often some of the least exciting stocks. And that is precisely their strongest selling point. With such a vast world of dividend growth stocks available out there, it is important to screen through to see if there are any worthwhile investments to explore.

They are stocks that provide growing wealth over time to income investors. Dividend growers are often larger (not always), more financially stable companies that can pay out reliable cash flows to investors. Some are slower growers than others. Some are going to be cyclical that require a strong economy. Some are going to be secular, which doesn't generally rely on a more robust economy.

Dividend growth can promote share price appreciation. Of course, that is if these companies are growing their earnings to support such dividend growth in the first place. Trust me. There are yield traps out there - I've owned a few that I'm not particularly proud of.

I like to think of investing in dividend stocks as a perpetual loan of sorts. Essentially, every dividend is a repayment of your original capital. Eventually, holding long enough, you have the position "paid off." It is all returned back into your pocket from that point forward.

All of this being said, it is important to understand my approach to dividend stocks and why screening dividend stocks can be important for income investors. As with any initial screening, this is just an initial dive - more due diligence would be necessary before pulling the trigger.

The Parameters For Screening

I'll be using some handy features that Seeking Alpha provides right here on their website for this screen. In particular, I will be screening utilizing their quant grades in dividend safety, dividend growth and dividend consistency.

Dividend Safety is relatively self-explanatory. These will be stocks that SA quants show reasonable safety compared to the rest of their various sectors. The grade considers many different factors, but earnings payout ratios, debt and free cash flow are among these. This category will be stocks with A+ to B- ratings.

For the dividend growth category, we have factors such as the CAGR of various periods relative to other stocks in the same sector. Additionally, the quants also look at earnings, revenue and EBITDA growth. As we will see, this doesn't mean that every stock with a higher grade has the growth we are looking for. This just factors in that the dividend has grown or earnings are growing to support possible dividend growth. For these, the grades will also be A+ through B- ratings.

Finally, for dividend consistency, we want stocks that will be paying reliable dividends for us for a very long time. In particular, hopefully, they are raising yearly, though that isn't an explicit requirement. We will also include stocks with a general uptrend in dividend payments, which means there could have been periods where they paused increases for a year or two.

After looking at those factors alone, we are left with 433 stocks at this time from the 423 listed last month. I'll link the screen here, though it is a dynamic list that constantly updates regularly. When viewing this article, there could be more or less when going to the link.

From there, I wanted to narrow down the list a lot more. I then sorted the list by forward dividend yield, from highest to lowest. Since these will be safer dividend stocks in the first place, screening for those among the higher payers shouldn't hurt. Though there are always exceptions and I try to note those.

Below, we go over a quick initial look at the top five highest rankings according to dividend yield. However, as is usually the case, we have some duplicates every month, and those that we have touched on recently that we'll be skipping over include NewLake Capital Partners (OTCQX:NLCP), NETSTREIT Corp. (NTST) and United Parcel Service (UPS).

We also have names that come up on the list that doesn't really necessarily meet the spirit of the screening piece in terms of consistent growth over time. This month, that means we'll be skipping over The Western Union (WU)—a company that has continued to struggle with newer competition and has now gone four full years without a dividend bump.

With that said, here are the names that we will be touching on today: Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR), Enbridge (ENB), Edison International (EIX), NNN REIT (NNN) and VICI Properties (VICI). Three of these names are REITs, and the other two are utility/infrastructure names. These are naturally names that come with relatively higher yields, and are certainly interesting being an income-focused investor.

Innovative Industrial Properties - 10.49% Yield

IIPR is a popular REIT in the industrial cannabis space, of which there aren't too many publicly traded options for investors. This is the only one that trades on the NYSE as well. It offers a tempting yield, which is similar to its significantly smaller peer, NLCP—which we touched on just last month. However, these companies come with higher relative risks in terms of regulations.

More specifically, IIPR has found itself in a bit of trouble with another tenant having issues and defaulting on its rent. Not just any tenant, either; it's the largest tenant in terms of the annual base rent. So that comes with a huge overhanging risk in terms of what they may have to do with the dividend going forward.

Though they also recently announced a resolution with PharmaCann, but have reduced cumulative total base rent and are working with PharmaCann to find new tenants for two of the leased properties. If no new tenant is found by August 1, 2025, then IIPR "will regain full control." The "monthly base rent of $1.3 million will be abated in full effective February 1, 2025."

The REIT had already seen its growth slow significantly, and that meant its distribution growth had slowed to a crawl. Of course, given the above, AFFO is expected to take a hit here. Analysts are estimating that 2024 AFFO will come to $8.04 and 2025 will decline to $7.72.

Based on the latest $1.90 quarterly, we would be looking at a payout ratio of almost 98.5% for 2025 if they deliver analysts' estimates. That's an incredibly tight coverage, and that could see them reduce the payout in order to keep some cushion for cash flow. If any further issues crop up in the near term, that almost nearly guarantees a dividend reduction.

I currently hold a position, but it certainly is a highly speculative one at this time if they can maintain the current dividend to investors. At the same time, some of the best performances that investors can achieve are in those names that present the highest risks. There has since been some recovery in the share price, so there appears to be some optimism here. Earnings are coming up on February 19, which should provide us with some further updates.

Enbridge - 6.17% Yield

ENB is an income-investor favorite, which needs little introduction, as this name pops up regularly on this monthly publication. This would be thanks to its long history of paying a consistently growing dividend. The company is located in Canada and is primarily known for its pipeline business due to the size of that business. However, it wouldn't be incorrect to label this company as more of an infrastructure company, as it works through several lines of business.

That includes a utility business that it has been working on growing through acquisitions, which have closed as of the latest quarterly results.

Our leverage is within our target range of 4.5 to 5x debt-to-EBITDA after closing PSNC, and we expect to trend down over the next few quarters. With the acquisitions closed and the funding completed, we have successfully concluded the acquisition of 3 U.S. gas utilities, which perfectly fit Enbridge's low-risk business model. And so we have returned to an equity self-funded model. This has been a well-executed major transaction that investors will benefit from for years to come.

For U.S. investors, it may not seem that dividend growth is consistent due to the CAD to USD conversion and the U.S. dollar strengthening.

However, they have been continuing to deliver dividend growth. In fact, the latest announcement was in December, where they provided a 3% bump; that took the dividend from $0.915 per quarter to $0.9425. They can also boast that they've paid dividends to their shareholders for going on 70 years now.

Edison International - 5.87% Yield

I had considered not including EIX because it is another highly speculative name currently in the utility space due to the Los Angeles Eaton fire disaster, where the company is being investigated. If it is found that they were responsible, we know how bad it can get for these utility companies. They then later admitted that their equipment may have sparked the fire.

More specifically, we have experience with California when the utility company PG&E (PCG) declared bankruptcy and reorganized due to liability claims for their role in a previous fire tragedy.

Although the equity here wasn't wiped out, which is unusual for a bankruptcy, shareholders certainly were hurting, nonetheless. The dividend was suspended and only brought back to a token penny in late 2023, and it was increased recently, but still remains a token 0.64% yield. That is even after PCG shares have been tanking recently, along with EIX's, due to the latest fires.

So, to be clear, this is a highly speculative position at this point, but they had otherwise been a steady dividend raiser, and the latest increase came with the last announcement.

They've set their next earnings announcement to take place on February 27, and investors might want to wait for more details at that time.

NNN REIT - 5.85% Yield

Speaking of defaults and bankruptcy, we arrive at NNN, which finds some of its tenants also in trouble. However, it isn't as serious of an issue since this REIT is overall more diversified, with these tenants being a smaller portion of the annual base rent. When talking about Frisch's and Conn's, here are some details they included on their previous earnings call.

Both of these tenants are on cash basis accounting, so we are only recording what we actually collect. Over the years, our guidance has generally assumed 100 basis points of rent loss in any given period but these 2 tenant issues could push us over that level, obviously, in the fourth quarter. We should get some incremental clarity on these 2 situations in the fourth quarter, which we think will allow us to better estimate the outcomes when we provide our 2025 guidance in February.

That was in the previous earnings call, but their latest earnings report showed that these issues were being resolved, which helped the shares rally higher post-earnings.

NNN has initiated eviction proceedings for 64 properties leased to a mid-western restaurant operator. As of December 31, 2024, NNN had taken back possession of 33 of those properties of which 28 properties have been re-leased to another restaurant operator with rent commencing May 1, 2025. NNN is working to take possession of the remainder of the properties in the first quarter of 2025. Additionally, during the fourth quarter of 2024, NNN took possession of 32 properties previously leased to a southeast U.S. furniture retailer that had filed for bankruptcy. As of December 31, 2024, NNN had sold six of these properties generating net proceeds of $21.8 million and re-leased five of these properties.

This is another name that I currently hold, and I consider it one of my core portfolio positions. I wrote some puts into this weakness to potentially capitalize on building out my position further, or generating some cash flow. These puts expire on March 21, 2025, and we selected the $40 strike price, collecting $1.39 in option premium (we originally sold these puts on January 3rd.)

NNN had already been trading well below its historical average range. That isn't that out of the ordinary for most REITs as we've shifted to a higher-rate environment. Due to persistently sticky inflation and tariffs, it looks like it'll be set to continue with rate cuts being minimal in 2025 after the Fed began cutting rates in late 2024. Still, the latest sell-off has pushed it further into a territory where I believe it is attractively valued.

VICI Properties - 5.69% Yield

Another name I currently own, VICI, is REIT, which owns experiential assets. They are fairly concentrated in terms of their largest contributors to the annual base rent. They primarily own casino properties, but they have been looking to branch out a bit more.

Fortunately, there don't seem to be any red flags going up on this one. At least, no specific tenant issues or investigations are taking place, as we noted with some others here. Though some investors may be disappointed in the lack of appreciation in the share price over the years, it continues to deliver a solid yield. Even further, during a recessionary period, most REITs are going to be susceptible, but the underlying businesses here aren't normally as impacted. During the Covid crash, AFFO here continued to grow at a steady clip.

This is a name we regularly utilize this name for the options wheel strategy to squeeze further cash flow from this name aside from collecting some dividends occasionally. That is, writing puts until potential assignment and then turning around and writing covered calls.
